passages.explanation: Saul approves of Stephen's execution. A great persecution breaks out against the church in Jerusalem, and everyone except the apostles scatters throughout Judea and Samaria. Devout men bury Stephen and mourn deeply. Saul goes from house to house, dragging off men and women and throwing them in prison. Ironically, the persecution fulfils Jesus' prophecy from chapter 1 — the believers scatter to Judea and Samaria, taking the gospel with them.

Philip — one of the seven chosen in chapter 6 — goes down to the city of Samaria and proclaims Christ there. This is significant: Jews and Samaritans had centuries of hostility between them. The crowds pay close attention to Philip because they hear and see the miraculous signs he performs. Unclean spirits come out of many, and paralysed and lame people are healed. There's great joy in that city.

Before Philip arrived, a man named Simon had practiced sorcery in Samaria and amazed everyone, claiming to be someone great. People from all social levels followed him, calling him the Great Power of God. They'd followed him for a long time because of his magic. But when Philip preaches the good news about the kingdom of God and Jesus, they believe and are baptised — both men and women. Even Simon himself believes and is baptised, following Philip everywhere, astonished by the signs and miracles he sees.

When the apostles in Jerusalem hear that Samaria has received the word of God, they send Peter and John. They pray for the Samaritan believers to receive the Holy Spirit, because the Spirit hasn't yet fallen on any of them — they'd only been baptised in the name of the Lord Jesus. Peter and John lay hands on them, and they receive the Holy Spirit. The apostles' involvement legitimises the Samaritan church and prevents an early split between Jewish and Samaritan believers.

Simon sees that the Spirit is given through the laying on of the apostles' hands and offers them money: give me this power too, so that anyone I lay hands on receives the Spirit. Peter's rebuke is fierce: may your silver perish with you, because you thought you could purchase God's gift with money. Your heart isn't right before God. Repent and pray that the Lord may forgive you for even thinking this. Simon is shaken and asks Peter to pray for him. This incident gives us the term 'simony' — buying or selling religious privileges.

Peter and John testify and preach the word of the Lord, and on their way back to Jerusalem, they preach the gospel in many Samaritan villages. The very apostles who once wanted to call fire down on Samaria (Luke 9:54) are now spreading the gospel there. People change.

An angel tells Philip to go south to the desert road from Jerusalem to Gaza. He obeys and encounters an Ethiopian eunuch — a high official in charge of the treasury of the queen of Ethiopia. The man is returning from worshipping in Jerusalem and is sitting in his chariot reading the prophet Isaiah. The Spirit tells Philip to go near the chariot. Philip runs over and hears him reading Isaiah 53. He asks if the man understands what he's reading. The eunuch's reply is honest and memorable: how can I, unless someone guides me?

The passage the eunuch is reading is Isaiah 53:7-8 — about someone led like a sheep to slaughter, silent before his shearers, whose life was taken from the earth. The eunuch asks Philip who the prophet is talking about — himself or someone else? Starting from that very passage, Philip tells him the good news about Jesus. Isaiah 53 is one of the clearest Old Testament prophecies about the Messiah's suffering, and Philip shows how Jesus fulfils it.

As they travel along, they come to some water, and the eunuch says: look, here's water — what's stopping me from being baptised? Philip baptises him right there. When they come up out of the water, the Spirit snatches Philip away — the eunuch doesn't see him again but goes on his way rejoicing. Philip appears at Azotus and preaches his way up the coast to Caesarea. The Ethiopian returns home with the gospel, tradition says he brought Christianity to Ethiopia.
